title: Outreach & Engagement: 2025 Year in Review - 211info
text: For our Community Outreach and Engagement Team, the core of the work is meeting communities where they are, listening first, and learning how to make it easier for people to understand how 211info exists to remove the guesswork. Through relationship-building, local presence, and targeted engagement, our team works to ensure that 211info is understood as a statewide resource serving all 36 counties in Oregon. Supporting Community Access Through Outreach In 2025, our Outreach and Engagement Team took part in 1,200 outreach activities . These included community events, partner meetings, in-person and virtual presentations, and print material distribution. These face-to-face moments help us connect with individual and underserved communities. Our outreach work does more than spread awareness. It also helps keep our resource database accurate and up to date. By working one on one with local service providers to update their records, we are ensuring their help reaches not just the most people, but the right people. This work is also essential to helping people know where to turn during disasters. During severe weather, wildfires, and other public emergencies, 211info serves as the central contact for information and connections to emergency resources. Solving Problems Together: Community Provider Roundtables To make the system work better, we have to talk to each other. Since launching in late 2023 , 211info’s provider roundtables have brought local community partners together to collaborate, share insights, and strengthen connections. In 2025, 211info held nine roundtables across 15 Oregon counties, engaging 164 community partners . Hosted by our regional Community Engagement Coordinators (CECs), these small, local conversations allow providers to share what they are seeing on the ground and help identify gaps in services, strengthening the network’s ability to meet the unique needs of each region Expanding Resource Access Through New Approaches In 2025, we also launched our first Resource Access Fair. This pilot effort was held in a community with limited access to services, but high demonstrated need, and brought together local providers and residents in one place to connect people more directly with available support. The event included 24 community-based organizations, and feedback showed strong support, with 89% indicating they would attend again. Events like these reinforce the value of reducing barriers to resources by pairing information access with in-person connection, especially in areas where systems can feel fragmented, such as rural communities with fewer options nearby. Looking Ahead By investing in local presence and listening closely to partners, 211info continues to strengthen its role as a connector across systems and communities statewide. As we move into 2026, we are building a team that reflects the diverse geography and culture across Oregon. This spring, we have provider roundtables planned in Douglas, Jackson, Klamath, Malheur, and Union counties . To receive an invitation, contact our outreach team at [email protected] . Together, we are building stronger, more connected networks of care that respond to the unique needs of communities across Oregon. Search Popular Posts Categories Equity News
